message:
fix(rpc/xdr): xdr_reference must zero-init via calloc for recursive procs

xdr_reference allocates a fresh buffer for caller-supplied recursive
procs (e.g., generated stubs for self-referential XDR types like
\`struct tree { tree *left; tree *right; }\`). The previous
\`libc::malloc(size)\` left the buffer uninitialized, so recursive
procs that read inner pointer fields (e.g., a \`next\` link) before
writing them saw whatever garbage was at that location and tried to
treat the value as an in-memory pointer.

Found by stress-running my own bd-xpgak regression test
\`xdr_reference_depth_limit_refuses_deep_recursive_wire_without_overflow\`
under --test-threads=16: SIGABRT with "misaligned pointer dereference:
address must be a multiple of 0x4 but is 0x3" inside
\`recursive_proc\`'s xdr_int call. The misaligned address was actually
the random byte pattern at the uninitialized \`next\` field offset
within the freshly-malloc'd RecursiveNode, which xdr_pointer then
read as if it were a valid \`(*pp).is_null()\` check.

Switch to \`libc::calloc(1, size)\` — mirrors the glibc xdr_reference
idiom which is malloc + memset(0). Inner pointer fields now reliably
read as NULL on the first descent, triggering the intended fresh
allocation path.

Verified: 5/5 cargo test --test xdr_abi_test --test-threads=16
iterations pass (was SIGSEGV/SIGABRT 4/5 times).

Note: this is the FIX for a real correctness defect, not just a
flake — any caller of xdr_pointer with a recursive proc on
self-referential types would hit this if the inner pointer-field
offset happened to land on a non-NULL byte pattern.
